title: Parahydrogen Cooling of Nuclear Spin Chains at Hypogeomagnetic Fields
authors: Alexey Kiryutin, Danil Markelov, Ivan Zhukov, Erik Van Dyke, Alexandra Yurkovskaya, Danila Barskiy
abstract: Solution-state molecular nuclear spin networks are promising quantum simulators because their scalar-coupling Hamiltonians are chemically programmable, precisely measurable, and coherent at room temperature. Their main limitation for quantum information science is initialization: thermal Boltzmann polarization produces highly mixed, high-entropy states. Here, we use parahydrogen-based Signal Amplification by Reversible Exchange (SABRE) at hypogeomagnetic fields (i.e., magnetic fields below Earth field) to hyperpolarize the chemically engineered 12-spin chain [U-13C,15N]-butyronitrile. SABRE generates percent-level 13C and 15N polarization and prepares non-equilibrium multi-spin orders across the network. A von Neumann entropy analysis of such a hyperpolarized system shows that, at the optimal transfer field of 0.52 uT, the full spin system could reach S/k = 8.274, compared with S/k = 8.318 for the unpolarized reference, giving (S-Sth)/k = -0.043. Experimentally, nuclear spin temperatures of 52 mK and 257 mK are achieved for 15N and 13C subensembles, respectively. The larger entropy deficit of the full network than of individual subsystems indicates correlated multi-spin order beyond single-spin polarizations. Rapid field cycling to 9.4 T enables site-resolved NMR readout, while the precisely determined coupling network provides an experimentally benchmarked Hamiltonian for testing quantum-simulation, quantum-control, and Hamiltonian-learning protocols.
summary: The research demonstrates that molecular nuclear spin chains can be initialized into highly ordered, non-equilibrium states suitable for quantum computation by employing parahydrogen-based Signal Amplification by Reversible Exchange (SABRE). By measuring entropy and achieving measurable polarization enhancements at room temperature, the authors establish a robust experimental method to prepare multi-spin registers for quantum control experiments.
main_problem: The primary challenge is overcoming the low initial polarization (high entropy) of molecular nuclear spin networks, which limits their use as quantum simulators for quantum information science.
main_result: The authors successfully hyperpolarized a 12-spin chain using SABRE at hypogeomagnetic fields, achieving non-equilibrium multi-spin orders indicated by a significant negative entropy deficit.
method: They employed Signal Amplification by Reversible Exchange (SABRE) utilizing parahydrogen ($	ext{pH}_2$) hyperpolarization transferred to the spin chain nuclei via reversible exchange at ultralow magnetic fields, followed by high-field NMR readout.
model_or_system: The system is a chemically engineered molecular nuclear spin network, specifically a ${}^{12}	ext{-spin chain}$ $[	ext{U}-{}^{13}	ext{C}, {}^{15}	ext{N}]	ext{-butyronitrile}$. The Hamiltonian dynamics are modeled as an analog quantum simulation of a Heisenberg spin chain.
key_observables: Von Neumann entropy ($S$), nuclear spin temperatures ($T_E$), and polarization levels for ${}^{13}	ext{C}$ and ${}^{15}	ext{N}$.
important_parameters: Optimal transfer field of $0.52\ \mu	ext{T}$; achieved spin temperatures of $52\ 	ext{mK}$ (${}^{15}	ext{N}$) and $257\ 	ext{mK}$ (${}^{13}	ext{C}$).
main_assumptions: The method does not cool the liquid sample itself but prepares a room-temperature ensemble whose quantum state is highly ordered relative to thermal equilibrium.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the SABRE process schematic, comparison of hyperpolarized vs. thermal NMR signals across various nuclei, and field dependence plots showing polarization buildup at ultralow fields.
paper_structure: The paper details the physical problem (low initial entropy), introduces the SABRE technique using $	ext{pH}_2$ at hypogeomagnetic fields to generate non-equilibrium order, quantifies this order via entropy analysis, and validates the method through site-resolved NMR readout.
why_interesting: This work provides a powerful experimental protocol for initializing quantum registers (spin systems) far from thermal equilibrium using chemical hyperpolarization, directly addressing a major bottleneck in liquid-state quantum simulation platforms.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Signal Amplification by Reversible Exchange (SABRE) as a method for pre-initializing nuclear spin chains. (A) Schematic of the reversible exchange of parahydrogen and a [U-13C,15N]- butyronitrile spin chain with an Ir-based polarization transfer complex. L and S denote ancillary ligands of the complex. (B) Polarization transfer from parahydrogen to the spin chain occurs through the network of scalar J-couplings within the complex, resulting in hyperpolarization of the spin chain. (C) 1H, 15N, and 13C NMR signals hyperpolarized by SABRE at 1 µT (top traces) compared with the corresponding thermal spectra, scaled by factors of 100, 1000, and 300, respectively (bottom traces). Note...

Figure 2: 15N (left) and 13C (right) NMR spectra (9.4 T) of [U-13C,15N]-butyronitrile obtained after SABRE polarization at ultralow magnetic fields (from −0.5 µT to +0.9 µT). The negative sign of the magnetic field indicates the opposite direction relative to the detection field in the NMR magnet. Note the multiplication factors of ×100 and ×5 for the data columns corresponding to pyridine (Py) and 2@Ir/2, respectively.

Figure 3: (A) Experimental (top) and calculated (bottom) dependence of signal intensities of SABRE-polarized signals as a function of ultralow magnetic field during parahydrogen bubbling. The bubbling time was set to 10 s for all experiments, and the temperature was maintained at 25 ◦C; (B) Dynamics of SABRE polarization buildup measured at magnetic fields of 0.4 µT and 2.0 µT for the 15N (top) and 13C (bottom) spins in [U-13C, 15N]-butyronitrile. All signal intensities are given as a percentage of the maximum theoretical polarization. The thin black lines show fits to the data using a mono-exponential function with the effective buildup time constant, τeff.
